issues.title: # of instances of AttestCertsAttribute
issues.author: hannestschofenig
issues.body:
> >>
> >>     A CSR MUST contain at most 1 AttestCertsAttribute. In the case where the CSR contains multiple instances of AttestAttribute representing multiple attestations, all necessary certificates MUST be contained in the same instance of AttestCertsAttribute.
> >>
> >>  
> >>
> >> Perhaps to make the wording easier, shall we change the description to “A CSR MUST only contain  exactly one AttestCertsAttribute”?
> >
> > Sounds like a good idea.
> 
> It's only a good idea if you ignore:
> 
> > [[MO]] But a CSR can also contain zero instances of AttestCertsAttribute, for example if you know that the RP already knows your public key then you have no need to transmit any certs. Alternatively, maybe there exists an Attestation format where the certs are buried inside the AttestStatement.
> > [[MO]] I could change the wording to “A CSR MUST contain 0 or 1 instances of AttestCertsAttribute” if that is clearer?
> 
> Given that you can't have less than zero instances of an attribute in a CSR, "at most one" is the same as "zero or one".   I wouldn't object to a change to zero or one, but I would to "exactly one".  If you always require exactly one, you will most likely be duplicating information that's available in the body of the actual attestation statement included in the AttestAttribute (cf Webauthn attestation formats).
> 
> Mike

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
I was coming here to open the same issue.

Tasks:
1. Re-word "at most one" to "zero or one".
2. Add text making it clear that the AttestCertsAttribute is optional and SHOULD be omitted if there are no certs to be carried, for example if the attestation key is already known to the verifier, or if the attestation statement format being carried already includes the cert chain, as is the case with WebAuthn -- see [WebAuthn-2 s. 8.2](https://www.w3.org/TR/webauthn-2/#sctn-packed-attestation).

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body: This has been addressed.

issues.title: Re-use of RATS’s Conceptual Messages Wrapper (CMW)
issues.author: hannestschofenig
issues.body:
Remark from Thomas:

> It looks very much like RATS’s CMW [1], except CMW builds on media types rather than OIDs to provide the typing information (and therefore does not need bootstrapping and maintenance of yet another IANA registry).
>  
> IMO it’d be better if we could converge on one only encap format.  Not only to avoid unnecessary duplication, but also because from the point of view of the RA/CA an AttestStatement is just a byte string that needs to be forwarded to their trusted attestation Verifier.  The RA/CA does not need to know which type of attestation statement it’s dealing with, as long as it knows that it’s a blob that the attestation Verifier will make sense of.
> 
> [1] https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ftbs-rats-msg-wrap-03#section-3.1


Proposal from Russ:
> 
> PKIXAttestIdentifier ::= CHOICE {
>    coap-content-format INTEGER (0..65535),
>    media-type IA5String }
> 
> CMOptions ::= ENUMERATED {
>    reference-values,
>    endorsements,
>    evidence,
>    attestation-results }
> 
>   PKIX-ATTESTATION ::= CLASS {
>       &id  PKIXAttestIdentifier UNIQUE,
>       &AttestType,
>       &cm CMOptions OPTIONAL
>   } WITH SYNTAX {
>       TYPE &AttestType IDENTIFIED BY &id [FOR &cm]
>   }
> 
>   PKIXAttestation{PKIX-ATTESTATION:PKIXAttestSet} ::= SEQUENCE {
>       attestID      PKIX-ATTESTATION.&id({PKIXAttestSet}),
>       attestType    CMOptions OPTIONAL,
>       attestValue   PKIX-ATTESTATION.&AttestType({PKIXAttestSet}{@attestID})
>   }
> 
> PKIXAttestSet PKIX-ATTESTATION ::= { ... }
> 

Suggestion from Thomas:

> I'd be more radical and just put the CMW as-is into an OCTET STRING.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
We should respond to this on-list.
The core question here is whether LAMPS / CSR should be specifying encodings other than ASN.1.

comments.author: thomas-fossati
comments.body:
> The core question here is whether LAMPS / CSR should be specifying encodings other than ASN.1.

LAMPS shouldn't specify core attestation formats, they already exist and are specified by other SDOs (e.g., FIDO, TCG, W3C) and IETF groups (e.g,. RATS).

The suggestion of using CMW (wrapped in a ASN.1 OCTET STRING) fully decouples attestation formats from the CSR format, making the latter scalable.

comments.author: nedmsmith
comments.body:
> We should respond to this on-list. The core question here is whether LAMPS / CSR should be specifying encodings other than ASN.1.

What is the rationale for redefining something that is already defined but doesn't use ASN.1 as the data definition language?

I makes sense to consider the constraints of the environments that are creating the CSR structures and their capabilities. HSMs that already support ASN.1/DER likely will object to some other encoding (e.g., CBOR), but environments that are already capable of CBOR may object to ASN.1/DER etc. Given there are use cases for CSRs originating from various environments such as DICE Protection Environment (DPE), SPDM Responders, and embedded uControllers (but not HSMs). Given there can be layers of compute that may issue a CSR, the CSR draft should be flexible toward accommodating industry evolution and trends rather than serve to constrain or limit such evolution.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: Don't bother now.  It will renamed when it is adopted.

issues.title: Freshness "out-of-scope" vs "not required"
issues.author: ounsworth
issues.body:
Since the document discusses it, it is by definition, in-scope.

Perhaps change the paragraph to say "is not required", or "is left up to the discretion of protocol designers and implementors" or something similar.

The whole paragraph about freshness probably needs a re-write for consistency since it currently reads like it was written by two authors (which it was).

comments.author: carl-wallace
comments.body: I don't think "not required" is what you want here but your second option seems fine. Maybe something like "while this specification does not impose any freshness requirements, an attestation format or certificate management protocol may impose freshness requirements".

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
MSJ:
> the freshness is less relevant, because we expect the protection of the key will never get worse (except for a compromise, which is not our problem today).

We could add wording to the security consideration to this effect -- ie that since we are asserting immutable / non-roll-backable properties of the private key's storage, freshness is less relevant.

That said, this feels like it's getting pretty deep into the semantics of a key attestation / platform attestation; which we are trying to avoid making assumptions about in this transport draft.

issues.title: Stand-alone use of the term "attestation"
issues.author: henkbirkholz
issues.body:
NIST decided support this definition:

https://www.nist.gov/itl/executive-order-improving-nations-cybersecurity/software-supply-chain-security-guidance-2

Using the term `attestation` in abstracts or titles becomes increasingly misleading. Maybe start with remote attestation and also use other less ambiguous terms or qualified terms from the beginning - even in the abstract.

issues.title: Change "CSR" to "Certificate Signing Request"
issues.author: henkbirkholz
issues.body: In this context, https://www.rfc-editor.org/materials/abbrev.expansion.txt expands CSR to Certificate Signing Request(s) and not Certification Signing Requests. For consistence, I'd also recommend to expand CA to Certificate Authority.

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body: And yes, I am aware of RFC 2986 and that it specifies `CertificationRequest` for PKCS#10. RFC 2986 also does not define CSR. Some terminology cleanup by summarizing that and then provide the reader with clear guidance on what means what can only be beneficial, I think.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: Only CA=> Certification Authority. Not the other words you used.

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body: So you think "Certification Signing Requests" is a real term that you use today? Did you look at RFCed's abbrev list?

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body:
RFC 4949 expands CA to "Certification Authority"
RFCed expands CA to "Certification Authority" or "Certificate Authority"

So => Certification Authority

RFC 4949 does not contain CSR
RFCed expands CSR to "Certificate Signing Request"

Would you like to redefine the RFCed listed (aka you can just write it in an I-D and do not have to expand it ever) meaning?

comments.body: Closing, as per LAMPS thread and corrections made to the text.

issues.title: Change AttestCertsAttribute it to SEQUENCE
issues.author: ounsworth
issues.body:
Comment from Carl Wallace:

> Adding an optional field adjacent to attestation type/value looks like it may be a good path forward (vs a sibling attribute as in current text). If you use a bag of certs in that context, SEQUENCE OF may be a better choice than SET OF as in the current attribute.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body: Counter-argument: a Key Attestation and a Platform attestation will likely share part or all of their cert chain, so having a single cert bag saves on duplication.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
Done. It's now a SEQUENCE:

```
ext-evidenceCerts EXTENSION ::= {
  TYPE SEQUENCE OF CertificateChoice
  COUNTS MAX 1
  IDENTIFIED BY id-aa-evidenceChainCerts
}
```

The other parts of this issue are covered by #28.

issues.title: ATTRIBUTE or EXTENSION to satisfy CRMF
issues.author: ounsworth

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
Specify in text:

> This Extension is intended only for use within CRMF CSRs and is NOT RECOMMENDED for use within X.509 certificates because publishing evidence carries privacy implications which are not considered in this document. See {{security-considerations}} for more discussion.

> # Security Considerations
> ## Publishing evidence in an X.509 extension
> This document specifies how to carry evidence in a PKCS#10 or CRMF Certificate Signing Request (CSR), but it is intentionally NOT RECOMMENDED for a CA to re-use that CRMF extension to publish the evidence in the resulting X.509 certificate. The reason for this is that the evidence might contain detailed information about hardware and patch levels of the device on which the private key resides. Certificates are considered public information. The certificate requester has consented to sharing this detailed device information with the CA but might not consent to having them published.
> These privacy considerations are beyond the scope of this document and may require additional signaling mechanisms in the CSR to prevent unintended publication of sensitive information, so we leave it as "NOT RECOMMENDED".

Especially in the Introduction, the current wording is sometimes ambiguous or conflicting and often not following RFC 4949 or RFC 9334. Also `attestation` is defined in https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-rats-tpm-based-network-device-attest-14.html#name-terminology-2

A few examples where the text is off: Evidence is verified or checked, a Target Environment is attested, at one point "a attribute" is defined, while it are two. Some phrasing can be made more fluid simply by restructuring sentences or making the language used more homogeneous.

issues.title: Optimizing the EvidenceBundle Structure
issues.author: hannestschofenig
issues.body:
The current draft (version -02) defines the EvidenceBundle as follws:

> 
>    EvidenceBundle ::= SEQUENCE
>    {
>      evidence  SEQUENCE OF EvidenceStatement,
>      certs SEQUENCE OF CertificateAlternatives OPTIONAL
>    }
> 

The idea is that Evidence can be sent with optionally with a certificate chain. In complex scenarios (such as Composite Devices) the sequence construct allows multiple Evidence payloads to be conveyed in a CSR. In such a situation it is possible to convey a certificate chain with each Evidence payload.

The payload construction would be something like:

               Evidence1
               cert1
               Evidence2
               cert2

We have also envisioned the case where the certificate chain is the same for all Evidence payloads. In such a case, it is possible to accomplish a performance optimization by only send one cert chain. Here is the payload construction:

               Evidence1
               Evidence2
               cert

If the certificate chains differ, then this optimization is not possible.

Mike StJohns brought up a scenario where the current design of the EvidenceBundle is limiting. In his scenario multiple Evidence payloads are sent and their certificate chain only differents by the end entity certificate. In the current design an optimization would not be possible.

Question: Should we allow this optimization? If so, how should we best design it?

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body:
As this PR touches issues #16 and #18, the first question that springs to my mind is:

Is it always Evidence (which from case to case can be very high volume) or also the complementary RATS conceptual message that is the Attestation Result?

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
> As this PR touches issues #16 and #18, the first question that springs to my mind is:
> 
> Is it always Evidence (which from case to case can be very high volume) or also the complementary RATS conceptual message that is the Attestation Result?

According to Fig 1, it is the Evidence from the Attester. Interesting idea that the CSR could also carry an Attestation Result. But I'm struggling to imagine how that would work; for example an RA could not replace the Evidence with an Attestation Result without breaking the signature on the CSR.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> As this PR touches issues #16 and #18, the first question that springs to my mind is:
> 
> Is it always Evidence (which from case to case can be very high volume) or also the complementary RATS conceptual message that is the Attestation Result?

I can't see how AR could be inserted by the Verifier into the CSR.
If the verifier (operated by the HSM manufacturer, or their representative) needs to communicate things other than pass/fail with the RP (the CA), then they need a different protocol.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
> I can't see how AR could be inserted by the Verifier into the CSR. If the verifier (operated by the HSM manufacturer, or their representative) needs to communicate things other than pass/fail with the RP (the CA), then they need a different protocol.

I expect we'll see a lot of "Pass/Fail for a given evidence verification profile" (or whatever the right RATS term is for that). Like "Does this CSR indicate compliance with CA/B BRs?", or "Did this CSR come from TPM-backed CAPI on a fully-patched Windows?". Or maybe even, the CA hands the verifier a list of evidence verification profiles (maybe each with its own trust store), and the Verifier hands back a list of profiles that this CSR complies with, the CA then smacks the corresponding OIDs on the cert.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> I expect we'll see a lot of "Pass/Fail for a given evidence verification profile" (or whatever the right RATS term is for that). Like "Does this CSR indicate compliance with CA/B BRs?", or "Did this CSR come from TPM-backed CAPI on a fully-patched Windows?". Or maybe even, the CA hands the verifier a list of evidence verification profiles (maybe each with its own trust store), and the Verifier hands back a list of profiles that this CSR complies with, the CA then smacks the corresponding OIDs on the cert.

Sure, but none of those messages can go INTO the CSR. Because it's signed by the Attester, and the Verifier can't modify that.
RATS does not define a protocol between RP and Verifier: we have to pick one or design one.
Your initial proposal that it's a proprietary popen("|hsm-manufacturer-module") is probably a good one for now.

pulls.title: Interaction between Attesting and Target Environment
pulls.author: hannestschofenig

comments.body:
#21 now uses "Certificate Signing Requests". Maybe:

```suggestion
A certificate signing request consists of a distinguished name, a public key,
```

PKCS#10 defines a message and it is called "Certification Request". This message has later been called "Certificate Signing Request".

In this paragraph I am referring to PKCS#10 -- maybe I should include a reference.

Correct, that is the ambiguity we have to resolve here, I think. But maybe not exactly here? Maybe somewhere earlier on - a quick recap of past evolution of terms and then an agreement on current use of terms in the document is the way to go?
Proposal is here: https://github.com/lamps-wg/csr-attestation/pull/27

reviews.author: nedmsmith
reviews.body: In general, it seems appropriate to follow RATS Arch capitalization for RATS roles and other terms having special meaning.

comments.body:
```suggestion
the following terms: Evidence, Claim, Attestation Results (AR), Attester,
```

```suggestion
Verifier, and Relying Party (RP).
```

```suggestion
be different from the Attesting Environment key used to sign Evidence about the Target Environment, which controls the private key used to sign the CSR. To
```

```suggestion
the Attesting Environment has to collect claims about this secure
```

Q: shouldn't the CSR Library be inside the Attester if not also inside the Target Environment that the Attesting Env measured as part of Evidence? Alternatively the CSR Library is a second TE that the AE measures.

```suggestion
 |    | Target Environment(TE)|       | |
```
Equally, a CRMF CSR is all those things with a set of extensions. We should mention both.

I don't know that that's true.
This is certainly the intent for the majority use-case, but on the last call, we discussed cases where evidence statement is unsigned and then the endorser would sign the CSR. Or ultra-contrained that can only contain one key and will need to cheat and use it as both the subject key requesting certification, and the attestation key.
... or CRMF CSR
Addressed in another PR.
Addressed in another PR.

Umm, fair question.
The way Hannes has drawn this definitely matches my understanding of the world. You want to keep your certified FIPS or CC boundary as small as possible; there's absolutely no reason to have your CSR-generation code inside it. 

Moreover, the CSR is probably constructed by CAPI or openssl or certbot or whatever application, and passed to the HSM over P11 as bytes to be signed. I wouldn't consider openssl or certbot running on a server adjacent to the HSM to be any sort of TE any more than nginx is a TE when it needs to use a TLS key stored on an HSM; I think it's an application making use of the HSM.

```suggestion
This document re-uses the terms defined in {{RFC9334}} related to remote
```

```suggestion
(CSR)" refers to a Certification Request message. While the term
```
Tad bit lost. Where has "it not been selected"? In RFC2986 (true) or in this document (not true). It was very much selected in RFC4949, which I'd add here.

```suggestion
it is not what has been selected. While the term "Certification Signing Request" and its corresponding acronym "CSR" are specified by {{?RFC4949}} and quite often used
in context of PKCS#10, it is equally applicable to other protocols
```

```suggestion
that use a different syntax and even a different encoding. In particular,
```

```suggestion
CSR messages.
```
I really would try to avoid using normative language in the SecConSec. If that is a strong requirement, it must come up earlier in the body of the text.
Are there cases in DICE where it could end up being the same key material?

The text above implies that the key-material for signing the CSR is in a target environment. Following that thought it would also imply that the signing code exists next to the key material in the same target environment.

It is, of course, an implementation decision of where to put what code. In the drawing I assume that the CSR library is outside the attester but the attester performs a software measurement of that code.

I will add a note to the draft.
I am not aware of such a requirement. Also, you are co-authors of RFCs, which did the same.
Yes, you could use the same key -- not just in DICE. From a key separation point of view that's not a good idea.
I don't know what "abt. TE" means

Well. RFC 9124 is like the odd one standing out (the SecConSec there is *the* relevant Section containing all the requirements next to the information elements themselves). I'd not use that one as an guideline.

```suggestion
environment (a.k.a., Target Environment, as shown in {{fig-attester}}).
```

> Are there cases in DICE where it could end up being the same key material?

I don't think so. DICE expects the Attesting Env will be different from the Target Env.

```suggestion
keying material. For the Verifier, the provided Evidence must allow
```

```suggestion
assurance about an Attester. Policies used at the Verifier and the Relying
```
"About Target Environment"
I'll retract this; I think it's a minor quibble.

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body:
A few questions:
* why the line breaks in the header?
* why adding some brackets in some places (e.g., Hannes' email address but not Mike's)
* what's the intended line break pattern in the kramdown part? (the intend could  be per paragraph, but then there would be quite a few exception to that pattern)

Personally, I'd prefer an editorial datatracker submission that would transition to a "per sentence" line break pattern & no only necessary line breaks in the header.

comments.author: meyer-chris
comments.body:
Fair points, Henk. And I must confess, For the brackets, I have no clue :-)
- Brackets seem to have been added by the auto-format function of my editor. 
- Line breaks sentences was something I removed to make Markdown exports more targeted if required, but nothing of major importance. If we want to do it differently fine as well, then we need to limit line lengths to 72 characters if I'm not mistaken.

Formatting is less important to me, the spelling fixes are something that we should merge, I reckon.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body: Note-to-self: I'll need to pull this locally and go through the diffs one-by-one.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
@meyer-chris This PR is 98% whitespace changes. I think I have extracted the meaningful content changes and submitted that as #43 .

If you're ok with it, I'd like to merge #43 and close this PR.

comments.author: meyer-chris
comments.body: Hey Mike, absolutely, feel free to go ahead! Fine by me... As mentioned, just a few typos

```suggestion
A client requesting a certificate from a Certification Authority (CA) may wish to offer believable claims about the protections afforded to the corresponding private key, such as whether the private key resides on a hardware security module or trusted platform module, and the protection capabilities provided by the hardware module. Including this evidence along with the certificate request can help to improve the assessment of the security posture for the private key, and suitability of the submitted key to the requested certificate profile. These evidence claims can include information about the hardware component's manufacturer, the version of installed or running firmware, the version of software installed or running in layers above the firmware, or the presence of hardware components providing specific protection capabilities or shielded locations (e.g., to protect keys).
```

comments.author: hannestschofenig
comments.body:
It is not a good idea to change the introduction when there is already another PR open that modifies the same text. This will result in lots of merge conflicts.

Additionally, there are open issues that need to be resolved and a re-write of the introduction is currently not an open issue. Note that the introduction has already been re-written several times.

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body: The language used was deviating significantly from RFC 9334 terminology and RFC 4949 terminology. The conflict with #22 was only about capitalization, which is noted in this PR. Rebasing it is not a problem. I'll file an issue.

comments.author: hannestschofenig
comments.body: Where does the terminology conflict with  RFC 9334 and RFC 4949 terminology?

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body:
A few prominent examples:

* Examples of major conflicts
    * w.r.t. RFC 9334, `This evidence is to be verified by a relying party`: "verified" means nothing in the RATS scope and Evidence is never appraised by a Relying Party - only by Verifiers
    * w.r.t. RFC 4949, `certificate chains` is a term deprecated at least for 15 years and `certification path` should be used.
* Examples of minor conflicts
    * `evidence signed by certificate chains` This makes no sense. An Attester signes Evidence and it probably should sign with appropriate key material that is endorsed via certification paths
    * to stay in sync with RFC 9334 (colloquial) language, it is preferable to write that RATS conceptual messages are `conveyed` and not `carried`

comments.author: hannestschofenig
comments.body:
I created two PRs to address the issues you have raised above instead of re-writing the text altogether. 

Three comments:

* Certificate chains is not a depreciated term. It depends what you want to say. 
* The terms "convey" and "carry" (or "transport") mean the same thing.
* The term "appraise" is something that aims to give the impression that there is something magic going on while in reality you are just comparing the provided data against some reference values. "Verification" is more natural.

comments.author: henkbirkholz
comments.body: These are only prominent examples and the text was littered with similar ones: I'd sincerely recommend to soldier through my proposal once instead of fixing each terminology nit one by one. I understand that reviewing takes time! But I think it is way more time consuming to comment on various related issues in several PRs. Like [here](https://github.com/lamps-wg/csr-attestation/pull/46#discussion_r1329808990) for example, where only some of the issues are addressed and a new issue is introduced.

```suggestion
This specification thereby is intended to be as technology-agnostic as it is feasible with respect to implemented remote attestation technologies. This specification focuses on the transport of Evidence via CSR while making minimal assumptions about content or format of the transported Evidence.
```
While RFC9334 does not define `attestation`, https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-rats-tpm-based-network-device-attest-14.html#name-terminology-2 does. Should we take that into account?
As I assumed that `different cryptographic algorithms` does not really add anything meaningful here, I removed it. Did I unintentionally remove meaning here?

```suggestion
This Evidence can be appraised by authoritative entities, such as a Registration Authority (RA) or a CA as part of validating an incoming certificate request against given certificate policy.
```

```suggestion
When requesting a certificate from a Certification Authority (CA), a PKI end entity may wish to include Evidence of the security properties of its environments in which private keys are stored.
```

```suggestion
This specification defines an attribute and an extension that allow for conveyance of Evidence in Certificate Signing Requests (CSRs) in either PKCS#10 {{RFC2986}} or Certificate Request Message Format (CRMF) {{RFC4211}} payloads.
```

```suggestion
While the term "attestation" is not defined in RFC 9334, it was later defined in {{?I-D.[ietf-rats-tpm-based-network-device-attest}}, it refers to the activity of producing and appraising remote attestation Evidence.
```

```suggestion
A Relying Party may consult an Attestation Result produced by a Verifier that has appraised the Evidence in making policy decisions about the trustworthiness of the
```

pulls.title: Example of encoding a TCG DICE CMW
pulls.author: ounsworth
pulls.body:
This PR's not super clean because it's sitting on top of the changes for #42.

The relevant change is this:

https://github.com/lamps-wg/csr-attestation/pull/44/files#diff-9b88f7a24d6eff606780b418b2e2d8ff3e7a400513a2249abd64b87c26686bcf

comments.body: You should put this back. The only reason I removed it was that I did not have SecureMimeMessageV3dot1 in the collection of ASN.1 modules I use to test compile. It is there though.

This one should be imported from:

```
  id-pkix
  FROM PKIX1Explicit-2009
      {iso(1) identified-organization(3) dod(6) internet(1) security(5)
      mechanisms(5) pkix(7) id-mod(0) id-mod-pkix1-explicit-02(51)}
```
Leave the TBDs for the spec. It's common practice to use fake values locally for test compilation purposes. The TBDs are needed by IANA.

comments.author: henkbirkholz

comments.author: hannestschofenig
comments.body: I gave it another try and shorted it a bit more.

```suggestion
A client requesting a certificate from a Certification Authority (CA) may wish to offer believable claims about the protections afforded to the corresponding private key, such as whether the private key resides on a hardware security module or the protection capabilities provided by the hardware.
```

LGTM!

Non-blocking suggestion: maybe `trustworthiness properties` instead of `suitability`?

```suggestion
Including Evidence along with a CSR can help to improve the assessment of the security posture for the private key, and the trustworthiness properties of the submitted key to the requested certificate profile. These Evidence Claims can include information about the hardware component's manufacturer, the version of installed or running firmware, the version of software installed or running in layers above the firmware, or the presence of hardware components providing specific protection capabilities or shielded locations (e.g., to protect keys).
```
